strengths: Structured claim-level schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges
Explicit abstention mechanism for conflicting or missing claims improves reliability
Ablation studies confirm causal role of LLM linker in performance gains
Efficiency improvements over A-Mem with 63.5% fewer tokens per question
Router composition strategies demonstrate practical deployment potential
weaknesses: Overall accuracy not statistically distinguishable from Flat RAG (p=0.21)
Low statistical power in paired tests for SSU (n=21) and abstention (n=9) slices
Regressions on assistant-authored, preference, and multi-session slices
Intent classifier for TTMG+R-LLM shows low recall on KU (39.1%) and MS (42.5%) slices
Claim schema's subject-predicate extraction may miss implicit reasoning patterns
required_revisions: Increase evaluation scale on capability slices to achieve sufficient statistical power
Improve assistant-authored claim extraction density through writer prompt adjustments
Implement multi-claim composition in reader to handle cross-session aggregation
Develop higher-recall intent classifier for inference-time routing
Explicitly address volatility class handling in the claim schema

strengths: Structured claim schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges addresses a critical gap in long-horizon agent memory
Explicit abstention mechanism provides verifiable truth-maintenance behavior, unlike flat RAG systems
Ablation isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains on targeted slices
Efficiency improvements over A-Mem demonstrate practical deployment viability
weaknesses: Key results on abstention and SSU slices are underpowered (n=9 and n=21 respectively)
Overall accuracy not significantly different from Flat RAG despite structural innovations
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS slices suggest limited expressivity of claim-level abstraction
Intent classifier for TTMG+R-LLM shows low recall on critical multi-session and knowledge-update slices
required_revisions: Increase sample size on abstention and SSU slices to achieve statistical power (n=30 and n=50 respectively)
Clarify whether the writer's session-batched processing introduces systematic bias against assistant-authored claims
Provide analysis of multi-claim composition failures in reader prompt (e.g., F3 cases)
Report F1 scores for router composition to better quantify precision/recall tradeoffs

strengths: Structured claim schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges addresses temporal consistency gaps in prior memory systems
Explicit abstention mechanism improves reliability on questions where information was never stated
Efficiency gains over A-Mem (-63.5% tokens) demonstrate practical deployment potential
Ablation studies isolate the LLM linker's causal role in truth-maintenance capabilities
Router composition preserves TTMG's benefits while matching Flat RAG's overall accuracy
weaknesses: Regressions on assistant-authored (SSA) and multi-session (MS) slices due to over-abstention
Statistical power limitations in paired tests on smaller slices (e.g., 9 abstention questions)
LLM linker's performance depends on prompt engineering rather than formal entailment
Intent classifier for TTMG+R-LLM shows low recall on MS (42.5%) and KU (39.1%) slices
Claim schema's subject-predicate framing may miss implicit user intent in preference questions
required_revisions: Expand evaluation on larger abstention and multi-session slices to strengthen statistical claims
Improve assistant-authored claim extraction density to address F1 failure mode
Enhance intent classifier recall on knowledge-update and multi-session slices
Add cross-claim composition mechanisms for multi-fact questions
Validate temporal parser robustness against LLM hallucinations in validity interval assignment

strengths: Novel claim-level memory schema with explicit validity intervals and truth-maintenance edges (contradict/supersede)
Clean experimental design: identical reader, embedding, and retrieval budget across systems
Strong abstention behaviour: 80 % explicit abstention vs 0 % for Flat RAG on the 30-question set
Statistically significant token-cost reduction (-63.5 %) versus A-Mem
Ablation study cleanly isolates the LLM linker as the causal driver of gains on every measured slice
Open release of code, prompts, splits, and per-question outputs aids reproducibility
weaknesses: Overall accuracy on the primary 150-question stratified slice is numerically lower than Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68.0 %) and not significantly different (p=0.21)
Small-sample power issues: key SSU and ABS claims rely on n=21 and n=9 subsets with McNemar p≥0.125
Router compositions still lose ~5–7 pp to an oracle upper bound, indicating limited coverage
Regressions on SSA/SSP/MS slices traced to over-abstention from narrow claim retrieval surface
Intent classifier recall on multi-session slice only 42.5 %, limiting practical router utility
Full 500-question replication deferred to camera-ready, leaving scale uncertainty
required_revisions: Run and report the full LongMemEval-S 500-question evaluation to confirm subset representativeness
Provide statistical power analysis or enlarge SSU/ABS slices to move McNemar results below α=0.05
Quantify and discuss the 6.7 pp overall accuracy gap versus Flat RAG—clarify whether this is an acceptable trade-off for abstention
Include confidence intervals or error bars on all per-slice accuracies in main tables
Release the exact claim-level and raw-turn retrieval contexts shown to the reader for a sample of questions to substantiate abstention decisions
Clarify hyper-parameter sensitivity (τ_hard, k_keep, similarity thresholds) with a brief sweep or ablation

strengths: Novel claim-level schema with validity intervals and explicit contradict/supersede edges
Rigorous ablation showing the LLM linker is causal for every measured slice
Full reproducibility package: code, prompts, seeds, raw per-question outputs
Efficiency win: −63.5 % tokens vs A-Mem with lower latency
Clean experimental protocol: identical reader/embeddings/budget across systems
Paired McNemar tests reported for every head-to-head comparison
weaknesses: Only 150-question stratified subset for main claims; power too low for several slices (SSU n=21, ABS n=9)
No statistical correction for multiple slice-wise tests (6+ comparisons)
No error bars or significance tests on token/latency table
Intent classifier recall on MS/KU only 42.5 %/39 %, yet used to route
No public code release link in paper; only promise of future upload
Flat RAG baseline never tuned (k, BM25 weight, reranker); may under-represent strong RAG
required_revisions: Run full 500-question LongMemEval-S and report pooled CIs; state sample-size calculation
Apply Holm–Bonferroni or similar correction for 6 slice-wise tests
Add bootstrap CIs or t-test on tokens & latency differences
Release code repo with tagged commit and SHA in reproducibility statement
Tune Flat RAG (sweep k ∈ {5,10,20} and BM25 weight) to ensure competitive baseline
Report variance across 3 seeds for stratified subset to show stability

strengths: Claim-level schema with validity intervals and explicit contradict/supersede edges is a clean abstraction for truth maintenance
Session-batched writer + lexical gate keeps write-time cost low (63 % fewer tokens than A-Mem)
Greedy max-consistent-subgraph retrieval is deterministic and O(k²) cheap at k=8
Explicit abstention policy gives interpretable “I don’t know” on 80 % of abstention probes
Router composition recovers Flat-RAG overall accuracy while preserving TTMG behaviour when triggered
weaknesses: Overall accuracy statistically ties Flat RAG (61.3 % vs 68 %, p=0.21) so headline win is narrow
LLM linker hyper-parameters (τ_sim=0.65, τ_hard=0.7) are hand-tuned on same 150-question slice → risk of over-fit
Intent classifier recall on multi-session only 42.5 %, leaving 15 pp on the table vs oracle
Writer skips assistant-authored claims (F1 failure mode) and multi-claim aggregates (F3) with no automatic recovery
Evaluation limited to 150-question stratified subset; full 500-question run promised for camera-ready only
required_revisions: Run the full 500-question LongMemEval-S and report 95 % CIs; include wall-clock ingestion cost per session
Ablate linker thresholds on a held-out 20 % split to show sensitivity/robustness
Add micro-benchmark of linker latency distribution (P50/P95) under peak session-arrival rate
Provide failure-rate analysis when subject/predicate lexical gate fires on noisy OCR or ASR input
Release open-source reference implementation plus indexing code to enable reproduction on new haystacks

strengths: Novel application of classical truth maintenance concepts to LLM agent memory with claim-level schema
Well-designed ablation study isolating the LLM linker's causal role across all measured slices
Honest and transparent reporting of failure modes and regressions (SSA, SSP, MS slices)
Meaningful efficiency gains: 63.5% token reduction vs A-Mem baseline
Explicit abstention behavior (80% on abstention questions) is a valuable trustworthiness property
Insight that claim-level and raw-text retrieval are complementary, motivating router composition
weaknesses: Overall accuracy numerically regresses (61.3% vs 68.0%) even if not statistically significant (p=0.21)
Key claimed improvements rely on underpowered statistics: abstention n=9 (p=0.125), SSU n=21 (p=0.50)
Full abstention set (n=30) shows no accuracy difference between TTMG and Flat RAG (both 76.7%)
Incomplete evaluation: only 150/500 questions from LongMemEval-S, full evaluation deferred to future work
TTMG alone insufficient—requires router composition to match baseline overall performance
Limited baseline comparisons (only Flat RAG and A-Mem); no comparison to other structured memory systems
required_revisions: Complete evaluation on full 500-question LongMemEval-S to validate generalizability of 150-question findings
Increase sample size for abstention and SSU slices to achieve adequate statistical power (current n=9 and n=21 are underpowered)
Add comparison to at least one additional structured memory baseline beyond A-Mem (e.g., MemGPT, MemoryBank)
Report effect sizes alongside p-values for all slice comparisons
Clarify whether the numerical overall regression (61.3% vs 68.0%) is a concern despite non-significance

strengths: Fair experimental setup: identical embedding model, reader, and retrieval budget across all systems
Rigorous statistical reporting: McNemar paired tests, bootstrap CIs, honest acknowledgment of underpowered comparisons
Clean ablation isolating the LLM linker's causal role with drops on every measured slice
Thorough failure-mode taxonomy (F1-F3) explaining regressions on SSA, SSP, MS slices
Evaluates on full abstention set (n=30) in addition to stratified subset, verifying generalization
Subset representativeness verified by running Flat RAG on full 500-question set
Detailed reproducibility statement with promised release of prompts, scripts, seeds, and raw outputs
weaknesses: Primary claims rest on underpowered tests: n=9 abstention (p=0.125), n=21 SSU (p=0.50)
TTMG not evaluated on full 500-question set; only Flat RAG verified for subset representativeness
Overall accuracy numerically lower than baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%) though not statistically significant
On full abstention set (n=30), TTMG and Flat RAG tie at 76.7%; explicit abstention behavior does not improve accuracy
Intent classifier weak (64.7% overall, 42.5% MS recall, 39.1% KU recall), limiting router effectiveness
Routers do not preserve abstention benefit; authors acknowledge as future work
A-Mem baseline is a reimplementation, not the original; fidelity not verified
Single stratified seed (seed=0); no cross-validation or sensitivity analysis over sampling
required_revisions: Run TTMG on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S to match Flat RAG's full-set evaluation
Report per-slice confidence intervals or standard errors, not just overall bootstrap CIs
Add multiple stratified sampling seeds to assess robustness of slice-level results
Include additional ablations: schema-only (no edges), retriever-only (no linker), to further isolate mechanisms
Verify A-Mem reimplementation against original paper's reported numbers or clarify implementation differences
Consider a stronger intent classifier or report results with oracle routing on each slice separately

strengths: Novel claim-level memory schema with explicit truth maintenance edges (contradict/supersede)
Significant cost reduction: -63.5% tokens vs A-Mem baseline (13603 vs 37260)
Explicit abstention behavior (80% vs 0%) is practically valuable for real deployments
Solid ablation study isolating linker's causal role across all slices
Deployable inference-time routers with proper statistical analysis (McNemar, bootstrap CIs)
Honest failure mode taxonomy (F1-F3) with clear future work directions
weaknesses: Overall accuracy numerically worse than Flat RAG (61.3% vs 68.0%), though not statistically significant (p=0.21)
Regressions on SSA (-35.3pp), SSP (-33.4pp), MS (-7.5pp) slices due to claim-level over-narrowness
Evaluation subset small (n=150); full 500-question run only for Flat RAG, not TTMG
Intent classifier poor recall on key slices (MS 42.5%, KU 39.1%) limits router effectiveness
Router composition does not preserve abstention benefit—routers use Flat's ABS accuracy
No real-world or production workload validation beyond synthetic benchmark
required_revisions: Run full N=500 LongMemEval-S evaluation for TTMG to enable direct comparison with Flat RAG's 70.0% baseline
Explicitly address why routers fall back to Flat on abstention questions and propose a fix
Report per-question latency breakdown (writer/linker/reader) to substantiate throughput claims
Consider denser assistant-side claim extraction to address F1 (writer entity gap) regression
Add analysis of memory growth rate and index size scaling with conversation length
